coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is widely used in disinfection, wastewater treatment, and chemical production. The current global market for H2O2, valued at over 3.5 billion USD, shows consistent growth. However, commercial method for producing H2O2, anthraquinone oxidation, relies on precious metal catalysts and generates massive organic waste. Developing clean methods for large-scale H2O2 production remains challenging. Due to natural abundance, biomass presents promising resource for H2O2 production through photocatalysis. While raw biomass and certain biopolymers lack semiconducting properties do not function as typical photocatalysts, carbon materials derived from biomass via hydrothermal carbonization and other methods have shown potential for photocatalytic H2O2 production. However, there is lack of comprehensive overviews on advancements and limitations of biomass-derived photocatalysts for H2O2 production. To address this gap, this review provides an in-depth discussion on the rational transformation of biomass and biopolymers into active photocatalysts capable of driving photoinduced oxygen reduction reactions to produce H2O2. Various strategies for the structural modification of lignin, cellulose, sugars, hesperidin, and biomass-derived carbon dots are examined. The existing challenges and potential future directions for further advancement in this field are also summarized. We believe this review offers valuable insights for developing efficient technologies for H2O2 production using biomass-derived carbon-based photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is widely used in disinfection, wastewater treatment, and chemical production. The current global market for H2O2, valued at over 3.5 billion USD, shows consistent growth. However, commercial method for producing H2O2, anthraquinone oxidation, relies on precious metal catalysts and generates massive organic waste. Developing clean methods for large-scale H2O2 production remains challenging. Due to natural abundance, biomass presents promising resource for H2O2 production through photocatalysis. While raw biomass and certain biopolymers lack semiconducting properties do not function as typical photocatalysts, carbon materials derived from biomass via hydrothermal carbonization and other methods have shown potential for photocatalytic H2O2 production. However, there is lack of comprehensive overviews on advancements and limitations of biomass-derived photocatalysts for H2O2 production. To address this gap, this review provides an in-depth discussion on the rational transformation of biomass and biopolymers into active photocatalysts capable of driving photoinduced oxygen reduction reactions to produce H2O2. Various strategies for the structural modification of lignin, cellulose, sugars, hesperidin, and biomass-derived carbon dots are examined. The existing challenges and potential future directions for further advancement in this field are also summarized. We believe this review offers valuable insights for developing efficient technologies for H2O2 production using biomass-derived carbon-based photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Since hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was first discovered by Thénard in 1818 through a chemical reaction between barium peroxide and nitric acid (Equation (1)) [1], it has garnered wide attention and is now recognized as one of the 100 most important chemicals worldwide [2].
(1) BaO2 + 2HNO3 → H2O2 + Ba(NO3)2

H2O2 is widely used in the pulp and paper industry, textiles, disinfection, wastewater treatment, as a feedstock in chemical production, in the preparation of personal care products, and in pharmaceutical drug formulation [3–5]. H2O2 It is preferred over other oxidants due to Ref. [6]its high oxidation potential (E0 = 1.76 V vs. RHE) and its environmentally friendly decomposition into harmless by-products—water and oxygen [1]. The high energy density of H2O2 (3 MJ L−1) makes it a promising candidate for fuel cell applications [7]. H2O2 as a liquid fuel, H2O2 offers advantages such as ease of handling, storage, and transportation compared to gaseous fuels [8].

Traditionally, the anthraquinone (AQ) oxidation method, developed by Riedl and Pfleiderer in 1913, has been widely used for H2O2 production (Fig. 1 a). Currently, nearly 95 % of H2O2 is produced using this method on an industrial scale [6]. This process has significantly boosted the global AQ market, which is expected to reach a value of 2.2 billion USD by 2025 [9]. Briefly, the AQ method involves the following steps [10]: alkyl anthraquinones are first hydrogenated in the presence of a supported Ni or Pd catalyst to produce the corresponding hydroquinones. A mixture of polar (alcohol) and non-polar (benzene) solvents is used to dissolve anthraquinone and its hydrogenated form. The hydroquinone formed in the second step undergoes autoxidation in air or O2 to produce H2O2, which regenerates the alkyl anthraquinone. The H2O2 extracted from the aqueous layer is concentrated via distillation. Despite its commercial success, the AQ method has several disadvantages: i) poisoning of Ni or Pd catalysts, ii) massive use of organic solvents, iii) gradual deactivation of alkyl anthraquinone over multiple cycles, iv) high energy consumption during H2O2 distillation, and v) indirect contribution to an increased CO2 footprint [11]. H2 gas, used in the hydrogenation step of the AQ method, is typically obtained from steam reforming methane (SRM), which releases 0.25 equivalents of CO2 per equivalent of H2O2 produced into the atmosphere [12].

Direct synthesis of H2O2 from H2 and O2 is another strategy for producing H2O2. Supported Pd is a well-known catalyst for this process, which proceeds via a proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) mechanism (Fig. 1b) [13,14]. Although the direct synthesis method avoids the use of toxic solvents, it has significant safety concerns due to the risk of explosion when mixing O2 and H2 gases [13,15]. Additionally, this method is limited by the high cost of catalysts and poor selectivity for H2O2 production, as competitive side reactions lead to the decomposition of H2O2.
(2) 2H2O → H2O2 + 2H+ + 2e− (E0 = 1.76 V vs. RHE)
(3) O2 + 2H+ + 2e− → H2O2 (E0 = 0.68 V vs. RHE)

The electrochemical route is an alternative strategy for H2O2 production (Fig. 1c). In this method, both oxidative and reductive pathways participate in the electrochemical production of H2O2 [16–19]. The oxidative pathway involves the two-electron (2e⁻) water oxidation reaction (Equation (2)), whereas the reductive pathway involves the 2e⁻ reduction of oxygen via the oxygen reduction reaction [4] (Equation (3)). Although significant progress has been made in developing efficient catalysts for electrochemical H2O2 production, this method faces challenges due to inferior performance caused by competing side reactions. The electrochemical oxidation of H2O to H2O2 competes with the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), decreasing the H2O2 production rate [20]. Similarly, the 2e⁻ ORR for producing H2O2 competes with the four-electron (4e⁻) transfer to produce H2O [21]. Various catalysts, such as noble metals [22], metal oxides [23], carbon-based materials [24–26], and single-atom catalysts [27], have been investigated for the electrosynthesis of H2O2.

In general, the electrochemical approach has slow kinetics for H2O2 production, poor selectivity due to side reactions, and non-metal-based catalysts exhibit a high overpotential to drive this reaction [28,29]. Moreover, the electricity required to drive these reactions is often derived from non-renewable energy sources, indirectly contributing to an increased CO2 footprint [30,31].To address these issues, artificial photosynthesis provides a unique opportunity to exploit solar energy, a free and abundant resource, for sustainable H2O2 production (Fig. 1d) [32,33]. A key advantage of this approach is the elimination of the need for external energy, and the use of earth-abundant photocatalysts ensures low production costs for generating H2O2 [34].

The Earth receives a massive flux of solar radiation (173,000 TW), far exceeding global energy consumption [35]. A major portion of the sunlight reaching Earth remains unutilized. Inspired by natural photosynthesis, where plants use sunlight as an energy source to produce carbohydrates, artificial photosynthesis offers an effective strategy for producing solar fuels and value-added chemical products [36]. In this regard, semiconductor photocatalysis provides a unique opportunity for solar energy conversion. Photocatalysis has attracted considerable attention since the pioneering work of Fujishima and Honda in 1972 [37], and it continues to be a significant area of research. The fundamental principles of semiconductor photocatalysis are as follows [38]: photons with energies equal to or greater than the bandgap excite semiconductors to generate electron–hole pairs. A fraction of the photogenerated electrons and holes may undergo radiative (emissive) or non-radiative (heat-producing) recombination [39]. Charge carriers with longer lifetimes that reach the surface can drive specific redox reactions, depending on the thermodynamic potential of the respective semiconductors. The various phenomena that occur during photocatalysis can be summarized as follows: i) light absorption, ii) bandgap excitation and the generation of electron/hole pairs, iii) bulk recombination, iv) surface recombination, and v) surface redox reactions (Fig. 2 a) [40]. Severe recombination is primarily responsible for the low quantum efficiency of photocatalytic reactions. Other drawbacks include insufficient light absorption and a gradual decrease in photocatalytic activity under prolonged irradiation, due to the poor stability of the semiconductor. These shortcomings limit the large-scale applications of photocatalysis [41].

Although several strategies, including bulk and surface modifications, have been explored, it remains quite challenging to completely suppress the recombination process. Further efforts are required to reduce charge recombination in order to achieve breakthrough performance for photocatalytic H2O2 production.

Photocatalysis has been extensively studied for solar fuel production, including H2 [42,43] and H2O2 production [44,45], CO2 conversion [46], and the photodegradation of toxic pollutants [47]. H2O2, as a liquid fuel and oxidant, is easier to handle and transport. The mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production over illuminated semiconductors is summarized as follows: If the thermodynamic energy levels of a semiconductor are sufficient, both holes and electrons can participate in H2O2 production. The band edge positions of semiconductors are crucial to driving this reaction [48]. For the oxidative pathway, the photocatalyst must have sufficient oxidation potential (1.76 V vs. RHE) to oxidize H2O to H2O2 [49]. Beyond this, the semiconductor should maintain high selectivity for H2O2 production over O2 evolution (Fig. 2b). A variety of catalysts have demonstrated impressive performance in directly oxidizing H2O to H2O2 under illumination [50].
(4) O2 + 2H+ + 2 e− → H2O2 (E0 = 0.68 V vs. RHE)
(5) O2 + e− → . O2 − (E0 = −0.33 V vs. RHE)
(6)
. O2 − + 2H+ + e−→ H2O2 (E0 = 1.44 V vs. RHE)
(7) 2H2O → O2 + 4H+ + 4e− (E0 = 1.23 V vs. RHE)
(8) 2H2O → H2O2 + 2H+ + 2e− (E0 = 1.76 V vs. RHE)

The reduction pathway for photocatalytic H2O2 production involves the reduction of molecular oxygen (Fig. 2c) [51]. To drive this reaction, the conduction band of the semiconductor should be more negative than the oxygen reduction potential (0.68 V vs. RHE) to produce H2O2 [49]. Additionally, the semiconductor should exhibit high selectivity for the 2e⁻ ORR to produce H2O2 instead of the 4e− pathway, which is a competing side reaction. The photocatalytic 2e− ORR can proceed via a direct route through PCET, as shown in Equation (4), or H2O2 can be produced indirectly through the formation of superoxide anions ( . O2 −), as shown in Equations (5) and (6) [49]. Ideally, the protons required to drive this reaction should be supplied by water oxidation (Equation (7)) [49]. However, if a semiconductor has insufficient oxidative potential to oxidize water, it is only suitable for driving the half-reaction of O2 reduction to H2O2 [52]. In such cases, the electrons needed to drive the ORR are generally supplied from organic molecules, such as alcohols or carboxylic acids, which act as artificial electron donors [53]. Due to thermodynamic limitations, many semiconductors are capable of driving only half of the oxidation or reduction reactions. Oxidative pathway involves selective transformation of H2O to H2O2 (Fig. 2d) Nevertheless, some semiconductors can produce H2O2 via both oxidation and reduction pathways if the valence and conduction bands are perfectly aligned (Equation (8))

The limiting factors for large-scale H2O2 production using semiconducting photocatalysis include unfavorable O2 adsorption, poor electron transfer, and insufficient selectivity to activate molecular O2 to form . O2 − [54]. Therefore, surface engineering of photocatalysts is crucial to enhance selectivity for H₂O₂ production and minimize competing side reactions. Metal oxides [55], g-C3N4 [56], sulfides [57], and metal complexes have been extensively investigated for H2O2 production [58]. Carbon, as a naturally abundant material, has significant potential for catalytic applications. Most studies on photocatalytic H2O2 production have focused on exploring metal-oxide semiconductors, and carbon-based photocatalysts, such as g-C3N4 and synthetic conjugated polymers, have also been investigated [59]. Beyond g-C3N4, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been exploited as carbon-based cocatalysts during photocatalytic reactions, including H2O2 production [60,61].

Biomass-derived carbon for photocatalytic H2O2 production represents an emerging and innovative strategy for harnessing solar energy. Biomass from natural resources can be converted into active photocatalysts using various strategies such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal treatment, or carbonization. The most common sources of biomass include crop residues, agricultural waste, spent coffee grounds, and food waste. Various biopolymers, such as lignin, cellulose, chitosan, hesperidin, glucose, starch, and fructose, can also be transformed into carbon-based photocatalysts. Biomass materials offer certain advantages, including natural abundance, low cost, environmental friendliness, and ease of chemical modification due to their structural diversity. The light absorption characteristics and charge carrier dynamics in biomass-derived carbon can be precisely tuned through rational structural modifications. Based on these characteristics, the tailored transformation of waste biomass into photoactive carbon for photocatalytic H2O2 production may open new opportunities for alternative fuel production and sustainable catalysis.

This review presents the key developments in the transformation of biomass into carbon-based photocatalysts for solar H2O2 production, highlighting the potential of this approach as an alternative to the AQ method. We systematically summarize different sources of biomass and structural modification strategies to design an efficient system. We also examine the existing challenges related to the efficiency and stability of biomass-based photocatalysts and propose potential strategies for the effective utilization of biomass for solar H2O2 production.

Plant-based biomass, with an annual production exceeding 170 billion tons, represents an abundant resource [62]. As a valuable source, it can be used to generate heat, electricity, and biofuels through technologies such as combustion, anaerobic digestion, and gasification [63]. Biomass-driven energy conversion is a promising approach to achieving a carbon-neutral cycle, as CO2 released during biomass utilization is counterbalanced by the CO2 absorbed by plants during photosynthesis [64]. Plant-based biomass is primarily composed of aliphatic polysaccharides—cellulose (40%–50 %) and hemicellulose (23%–32 %)—and aromatic polymers, such as lignin (10%–25 %) [65]. An overview of the distribution of biopolymers in plant-based biomass is provided in Fig. 3 a [66]. Cellulose is the most abundant natural polymer on Earth [67], while lignin, with its high carbon content, is the second most abundant biopolymer [68]. The paper and pulp industry generates approximately 50 million tons of waste lignin [69], which is not properly utilized. Although lignin is insoluble in aqueous solutions, its water-soluble form, lignosulfonate [14], can be produced by sulfonating lignin through treatment with sulfuric acid [69]. Another biomass type is chitosan, a polysaccharide that is abundantly present in seafood [70].

Using biomass as a photocatalyst precursor offers several advantages over traditional inorganic materials, such as low cost, high availability, and biocompatibility [71]. Biomass-derived photocatalysts possess some unique benefits: (i) biomass is an abundantly renewable and sustainable resource, unlike the scarce minerals used for inorganic photocatalysts; (ii) biomass-derived photocatalysts do not generate toxic by-products, unlike chemically synthesized photocatalysts; and (iii) biopolymers are enriched with carbon and oxygen, which makes them easy to functionalize [72]. Additionally, biomass-based photocatalysts offer more active sites due to the presence of naturally available heteroatoms, which can enhance photocatalytic performance for H2O2 production [73–76]. Given these advantages, biomass and polymers have emerged as promising candidates for photocatalysis. Blending biomass with other photocatalysts can further enhance their photocatalytic activities [77]. However, the full potential of biomass in photocatalysis has not been realized due to certain challenges [78]. For instance, pristine biomass derived from plants has limited activity in driving photoredox reactions, mainly because of its insulating nature and the lack of semiconducting properties in biomass and biopolymers. Developing rational technologies to exploit biomass as a photocatalyst can open new opportunities for converting waste into valuable resources. Recently, various strategies have been reported for transforming biomass into light-absorbing materials with semiconducting properties [79]. Chemical modification strategies may improve the efficiency of biomass-based photocatalysts for H2O2 production.

Biomass-derived photocatalysts have primarily been studied for H2 production and waste remediation, while less attention has been given to biomass-based photocatalysts for H2O2 production [80]. Given the significance of H2O2 as a valuable fuel with high energy density and as a clean oxidant, developing biomass-derived photocatalysts for H2O2 production is highly desirable. The conventional method for producing biofuels from biomass involves high-temperature pyrolysis to remove the oxygen content of lignin, which increases production costs. Interestingly, the high oxygen content of lignin is advantageous for photocatalytic H2O2 production due to the favorable contribution of oxygenic functional groups in the ORR [81]. Oxygenic functional groups can accelerate ORR kinetics to produce H2O2. The distribution of oxygenic functionalities in biomass-derived carbon can be tuned by adjusting thermal treatment conditions, as shown in Fig. 3b [82]. The following discussion provides a systematic overview of recent developments focusing on various strategies adopted for transforming biomass into photocatalysts for H2O2 production. Various types of biomass and biomass-derived polymers, such as starch, lignin, chitosan, cellulose, and hesperidin, have been transformed into optically active catalysts and hybridized with secondary materials to tailor their activities for solar H2O2 production. An overview of the biomass and biomass-based polymers utilized thus far along with modification strategies to transform them into active photocatalyst for H2O2 production is presented in Fig. 4 . The timelines for the development of biomass-driven photocatalysts for H2O2 production are highlighted in Fig. 5 . Miglbauer et al. were the first to demonstrate the simultaneous valorization of lignin and H2O2 production by studying both lignin and LS [83]. Lignin, being insoluble in aqueous environments, acts as a heterogeneous catalyst to drive the redox reaction, whereas LS, soluble in water, functions as a homogeneous catalyst. This study employed electron donors (oxalate, formate, and glucose) to compete for the self-oxidation of lignin and LS. For LS, oxalate as an electron donor produced the highest efficiency for H2O2 generation, reaching a maximum of 2400 μM after 8 h of irradiation (Fig. 6 a). In comparison, the effects of formate and glucose as electron donors were less significant, resulting in inferior H2O2 production (1100 μM). Fig. 6b demonstrates that the self-oxidation of LS was significantly influenced by the nature of the electron donor, with oxalate accelerating the decomposition of LS. Notably, when no electron donors were used, H2O2 evolution remained lower (1100 μM for LS under acidic pH).

The absence of electron donors also suppresses LS degradation, confirming that H2O2 is obtained at the expense of LS during self-oxidation. Fig. 6c shows that the efficiency of lignin in producing H2O2 was significantly lower than that of LS. A schematic diagram of the concurrent production of H2O2 and degradation of lignin or LS is shown in Fig. 6d. This study provides a unique opportunity for biomass valorization coupled with H2O2 formation as a value-added product. However, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of lignin-based thin films exhibits a serious drawback, as they generate a negligible photocurrent density due to the high electrical resistance of the fabricated electrodes. This indicates that lignin-based electrodes must be modified to improve electrical conductivity and, consequently, the PEC performance.

Recently, Kim et al. explored the modification of lignin, LS, and kraft lignin [80] to produce H2O2 through both water oxidation to H2O2 and O2 reduction under illumination without using electron donors [69]. In situ-generated H2O2 was coupled with peroxygenases for the photo-enzymatic functionalization of stable C–H bond activation, introducing oxy groups (hydroxyl and epoxide). The overall organic transformation reaction driven by the generated H2O2 is shown in Fig. 7 a. Compared to LS, KL demonstrated a higher efficiency for H2O2 production (160 ± 30 mM h−1 g−1) (Fig. 7b).

LS solution irradiated over 5 h under acidic and neutral conditions, with different electron donors; (b) LS concentration over irradiation time, calculated spectrophotometrically; (c) H2O2 over time measured for spin-coated lignin films under the same set of conditions; and (d) schematic of photochemical reduction of dissolved oxygen on lignin or LS (left). The photograph on the right shows the simple photochemical experiment with lignin deposited on polyethylene terephthalate [63] and LS in solution, non-irradiated and irradiated with a violet light source. Adopted from Ref. [83] The superior response of KL was attributed to the enhanced charge separation and better collection efficiency, supported by the higher photocurrent response of KL, as shown in Fig. 7c. Interestingly, lignin-based polymers, such as cellulolytic lignin and lignin dehydrogenation polymer (DHP), also produce H2O2, indicating the universality of lignin-based catalysts for catalyzing H2O2 production.

The catalytic performance of LS and KL was quantified by measuring the turnover number (TTN). Fig. 7d shows that the TTN observed for LS (81,000) for the hydroxylation of ethylbenzene under visible illumination was significantly higher than that of other photocatalysts using water as an electron donor. Other photocatalysts, such as Au/TiO2 and g-C3N4, require the use of organic electron donors, and their TTN remains low compared to LS and KL. Notably, OH radicals and lignin can effectively inhibit peroxygenase deactivation due to their antioxidant and scavenging properties. Due to the suitable band position and photooxidation ability of lignin-based biopolymers, no electron donors were needed for C–H bond functionalization.

This study highlights the potential of utilizing lignin biomass for biocatalytic organic transformations. Jang et al. have exploited artificial wood powder, composed of lignin and cellulose, for H2O2 production [84]. Aggregation of lignin was suppressed due to hydrogen bonding interactions between lignin and cellulose. The artificial wood developed in this work has demonstrated promising efficiency for photocatalytic H2O2 production and has been coupled with the bio-catalyst peroxygenase for an oxy-functionalization reaction. The overall process is highlighted in the scheme provided in Fig. 8 a.

The homogeneous variation of C=C in the artificial wood, as compared to lignin, observed in Fig. 8b, confirms that the former has better dispersion with minimized aggregation. This significantly influences the corresponding activities for H2O2 production over lignin and artificial wood, as shown in Fig. 8c. It was observed that the H2O2 production efficiency gradually decreased as the lignin concentration increased from 1 mg L−1 to 10 mg L−1, likely due to lignin aggregation and blocking of the catalyst's active sites. However, the artificial wood exhibited a gradual improvement in H2O2 production rate with increasing concentration, reflecting better dispersibility and suppressed aggregation. A similar trend was observed for the oxy-functionalization reaction, as shown in Fig. 8d.

Moreover, the TTNs for oxy-functionalization achieved by artificial wood outperformed those of TiO2 and C3N4-based photocatalysts. This work highlights an important strategy for utilizing lignin-based biomass for in-situ production of H2O2 and its application in organic synthesis. Gopakumar et al. studied the use of biomass as a support material for producing a hybrid photocatalyst (biomass-BiOBr) to generate H2O2 using seawater, referred to as LBOB. Fig. 9 a shows the model of lignin in interaction with BiOBr. The performance for H2O2 production over LBOB was compared to chitosan (CBOB), graphene hybrids with BiOBr (GBOB), and pristine BiOBr (BOB) [81]. Band structure analysis of these catalysts confirms their suitability to drive the oxygen reduction reaction for H2O2 production via a direct route, which involves PCET.

In contrast, the indirect route involves a sequential two-electron transfer through the formation of . O2 −. For LBOB, the synergistic effect of both the direct and indirect routes contributed to efficient H2O2 production. NaCl (0.6 M) was added to pure water to mimic the salinity of seawater. Pristine BOB and BOB hybrids with graphene and chitosan produced significantly lower amounts of H2O2. However, the lignin support was helpful in tuning the bandgap of the LBOB hybrid catalyst. The highest conduction band of LBOB (0.03 V vs. RHE) provided favorable conditions for producing H2O2. Various additives, including inorganic salts such as CaCl2, MgCl2, KCl, Na2SO4, acids (HCl, H2SO4), alkali (NaOH), and organic electron donors (HCOOH, CH3COOH, and C2H5OH), were added to pure water to examine their influence on H2O2 production over the LBOB. The corresponding results, shown in Fig. 9b, indicate that formic acid exhibits a superior response as an electron donor, enhancing the rate of H2O2 production. The performance of the LBOB photocatalyst in real seawater confirmed its sustained activity for H2O2 production, reflecting the stability of the catalyst under prolonged illumination (Fig. 9c). The use of seawater, as an abundant resource mixed with various electrolytes, provides a unique opportunity for clean energy production. Photocatalysts are usually deactivated in seawater because of its high salinity and the presence of organic matter. However, LBOB performed well in the production of H2O2 in the presence of inorganic electrolytes and natural seawater. This finding demonstrates the possibility of producing biomass hybrids with other photocatalysts to further increase photocatalytic activity for H2O2 production. This demonstrated the potential of seawater as a natural resource for H2O2 production.

Hesperidin (HP) is a biopolymer extracted from waste biomass of citrus fruits. Recently, Liu et al. showed a very simple approach of fabricating carbon dots (CDs) by hydrothermal treatment of hesperidin for producing H2O2 from water under illumination [85]. Hesperidin hydrothermally treated in the presence of H2SO4 at 150 °C for 2 h was referred to as HP-2. During the hydrothermal treatment, the morphology of hesperidin changed greatly. The TEM image in Fig. 10 a shows the sheet-like morphology of HP-2, which is quite different from that of HP-0, which has a rod structure. Furthermore, the HRTEM image in Fig. 10b confirms that the hydrothermal treatment produces CDs over sheet-like structures. This influences the charge-carrier dynamics and photochemical properties. The formation of CDs suppressed the recombination of charge carriers, as is evident from the elongated lifetime of HP-2 (Fig. 10c). Ultimately, it also influenced the photocurrent response, which was greatly enhanced for HP-2 compared with that for HP-0, as shown in Fig. 10d. Without using electron donors, H2O2 production efficiency of CDs reached 4480 μmol L−1 (Fig. 10e). The charge extraction ability and H2O2 production efficiency of the CDs were dependent on the duration of the hydrothermal treatment. Among the different hydrothermal treatment durations, the samples prepared for 2 h showed the best H2O2 production. O2 production was confirmed by studying the half-reaction of water splitting for O2 evolution in the presence of AgNO3 as a sacrificial reagent. This confirms that the valence-band position of HP-2 favored water oxidation and eliminated the need for electron donors. Although HP-2 exhibited stable performance in repeated testing, the apparent quantum yield (AQY) for H2O2 production remains low. Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis, given in Fig. 10f, confirms that the formation of . O2 − as an intermediate actively participates in the process of H2O2 production. Electron donors are frequently used for photocatalytic H2O2 production because of their poor ability to catalyze water oxidation. However, known examples of donor-free H2O2 synthesis are limited. Hydrothermal carbonization is a versatile strategy to make hydrothermal carbons (HTC). Hydrothermal carbonization may introduce semiconducting properties to biomass-derived carbon, which can facilitate driving photo-redox reactions [86–91]. Xu et al. demonstrated various photocatalysts derived from biomass for H2O2 production using water as an electron donor [92]. Panchromatic light-absorbing catalysts (visible and IR regions) were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of glucose, sucrose, starch, cellulose, and waste paperboard to prepare an active photocatalyst referred to as hydrothermal carbonaceous carbon (HTCC). Fabrication of HTCC is shown in Fig. 11 a. For comparison, C3N4 was prepared and tested as a control sample. Fig. 11b and c shows that HTCC and C3N4 have quite similar values of flat band potential; however, HTCC has much better absorption in the visible region. High-temperature hydrothermal transformations can introduce semiconducting features to biomass-derived carbon, making it active in photo-driven redox reactions. The use of photons of longer wavelengths enabled HTCC to accelerate H2O2 production, which was much higher compared to the activity observed for C3N4, a well-known photocatalyst, as shown in Fig. 11d. The highest efficiency observed for cellulose-derived HTCC reached 1.16 mmol gcat −1 h−1, with a AQY of 18.2 % at 420 nm. One of the key reasons for the poor quantum efficiencies of photocatalysts for H2O2 production is the rapid recombination and ineffective separation of charge carriers. Theoretical calculations revealed that the furan moieties in HTCC can play an important role in activating dioxygen and promoting charge transfer phenomena. Fig. 11e reflects acceleration in H2O2 formation over HTCC under O2 saturation and its suppression under N2 environment. This confirms O2 reduction as the dominating pathway for H2O2 production. A significant reduction in H2O2 production in the presence of p-benzoquinone highlights that ORR was dominantly proceeding through the formation of . O2 −. This was further confirmed by measuring spin-trapped ESR, given in Fig. 11f. DFT calculations have revealed penta-cycles of HTCC as an active site for O2 activation, as shown in Fig. 11g. The charge density map, given in Fig. 11h, further identifies donor and acceptor. The overall mechanism producing H2O2 over HTCC and the intermediate generation of . O2 − is highlighted in the schematic diagram given in Fig. 11i. HTCC effectively suppressed the photodecomposition of H2O2. They also demonstrated good stability under prolonged illumination for the production of H2O2. This study opens new possibilities for exploiting cellulose and polysaccharide-based biomasses for H2O2 production.

Miao et al. have adopted a hydrothermal strategy to transform polysaccharides and waste biomass materials to fabricate 12 different types of HTCs [93]. The process of fabricating HTC is highlighted in Fig. 12 a. A notable feature of these HTCs is the existence of aromatic and quinoid furan structures, enabling the formation of a donor-acceptor configuration. The existence of furan structures was confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 12b. These furan structures have facilitated efficient transfer of photoinduced charge carriers among HTCs, promoting the photoinduced ORR for efficient production of H2O2 without adding electron donors. The highest efficiency for H2O2 production over HTC has been observed as 480.7 μmol gcat −1 h−1. An overview of H2O2 production rate over HTC has been summarized in Fig. 12c. The order of increasing H2O2 production over biomass-based HTC produced from cellulose, bean straw, and rice straw was observed as C-HTC > B-HTC > R-HTC. This work demonstrates the structure-activity relations of biomass-derived photocatalysts for H2O2 production. It also demonstrates the potential of applying abundantly available and affordable raw materials for large-scale synthesis of H2O2. Zhang et al. prepared a pyrrole-containing hydrothermal carbon (N-SHTC) using starch and nitric acid as precursors. Nitric acid effectively achieved hydrothermal carbon at a lower elevated temperature [94] and facilitated nitrogen doping into furan-based structures, forming the donor-acceptor complex, as shown in Fig. 13 a. For comparison, starch-derived hydrothermal carbon without nitric acid (SHTC) was prepared and analyzed, enabling a critical examination of the influence of pyrrolic nitrogen on the structure and activity. The introduction of nitrogen into the furan structure of N-SHTC was confirmed using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 13b) where the vibrations observed at 1255 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C–N. C1s spectra, given in Fig. 13c, indicate no significant change in the carbon structure, with peak fittings at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.3 eV being assigned to C–C/C=C, C–O, and C=O, respectively. The N1s spectra in Fig. 13d confirm that the nitrogen in N-SHTC predominantly exists in the pyrrolic form. The supportive role of nitrogen doping was found to enhance N-SHTC's ability to boost the rate of H2O2 production in water, reaching 1132 μmol g−1 h−1, significantly higher than pristine SHTC without nitrogen doping, as shown in Fig. 13e. The superior performance of N-SHTC was maintained by introducing additive salts such as LiCl, NaCl, KCl, and MgCl2. The influence of quenchers on the ability of N-SHTC to produce H2O2 is illustrated in Fig. 13f. A significant decrease in H2O2 production in the presence of benzoquinone and AgNO3 confirmed that . O2 − was a major intermediate and that oxygen reduction was the primary pathway during H2O2 production (Fig. 13f).

The effect of the introduction of salts on the photoelectrochemical response was also investigated. Among the various salts, KCl exhibited the highest photocurrent response (Fig. 13g), likely enhancing the charge-transfer capability of the nitrogen-containing pyrrolic units. The current findings underscore the advantages of introducing heteroatoms into hydrothermally prepared carbon to enhance its catalytic performance for H2O2 production. In a recent study, Lu et al. demonstrated the hydrothermal transformation of organophosphorus and biomass to design a phosphorous-incorporated hydrothermal carbon catalyst (P-HTCC) for the efficient production of H2O2 [95]. This approach is significant for utilizing waste biomass and phosphorous-containing pesticides to produce value-added chemicals through solar radiation. The D. dichotoma and straw-based biomasses used in this study are depicted in Fig. 14 a and b. A schematic of the P-HTCC fabrication via the hydrothermal approach is shown in Fig. 14c.

In this study, the critical role of phosphorus was identified regarding its influence on the electronic band structure and optoelectronic properties. Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy and Mott-Schottky analysis revealed the bandgap and flat-band potential. It was observed that P-HTCC has a more negative conduction band position (−0.52 V vs. RHE) compared to that of HTCC (−0.25 V vs. RHE), as shown in Fig. 14d. This more cathodic positioning of the flat-band potential of P-HTCC resulted in a higher oxygen reduction ability for producing H2O2. Unlike many conventionally used semiconductor catalysts, such as C3N4, which often require sacrificial electron donors for H2O2 production during photocatalysis [96], P-HTCC can generate H2O2 in pure water without the need for organic electron donors. P-HTCC produced 920 μmol h−1 g−1, which is double the performance observed for HTCC, as shown in Fig. 14e. Moreover, the performance efficiency of P-HTCC obtained from cellulose remained higher than that from other sources, such as glucose and real rice straw-based biomass. The 13C solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum shown in Fig. 14f confirms the incorporation of phosphorus into P-HTCC, as evidenced by the C–P peak. DFT calculations were performed to investigate the binding strength of the intermediate between HTCC and P-HTCC. The greater decrease in Gibbs free energy in the case of P-HTCC reflects energetically favorable conditions for H2O2 production compared to HTCC, as shown in Fig. 14g.

This study will be valuable for exploiting agricultural waste containing organophosphates, although challenges remain to scale this process to a larger scale. Yao et al. designed a melanoidin catalyst from glucose and urea via pyrolysis, as shown schematically in Fig. 15 a [97]. It is composed of furan-pyrrole units that act as donor-acceptor groups, facilitating the extended separation of charge carriers. The temperature variation in the hydrothermal carbonization influenced the electronic structure of the catalyst, as observed through Mott-Schottky analysis. The conduction bands of ME200, ME250, and ME300 were observed at −0.54 V, −0.43 V, and −0.46 V (vs. RHE), respectively, as shown in Fig. 15b. The hydrothermal temperature also influenced the dynamics of the photoexcited charge carriers. The average lifetimes for ME200, ME250, and ME300 were observed as 6.89 ns, 18.96 ns, and 20.06 ns, respectively (Fig. 15c). The extended lifetime of the carriers enhanced H2O2 production.

ME250, prepared at 250 °C, exhibited optimal performance for photocatalytic H2O2 production, reaching a maximum of 330 μM g−1 h−1 without the need for any electron donor. Mechanistic investigations using ESR spectroscopy and radical detection confirmed that the ORR is the dominant pathway for H2O2 production, facilitated by the water oxidation reaction, which generates oxygen. Fig. 15d shows that using different nitrogen and carbon sources by replacing urea and glucose yielded a reasonable performance. Notably, melanoidin catalyst synthesized from bread also produced H2O2 at a rate of 80 μM g−1 h−1.

In addition to the hydrothermal carbonization of biomass polymers to convert them into active photocatalysts, these polymers have also been integrated with other semiconductors to enhance their catalytic activity for H2O2 production. One such semiconductor, C3N4, is well known for photocatalytic H2O2 production; however, it suffers from poor yield, low quantum efficiency, and the requirement for electron donors due to its insufficient water-oxidizing ability.

In a recent study, Shan et al. integrated C3N4 with carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), which has high hydroxyl content (OH) [98]. The fabrication scheme of the hybrid catalyst is shown in Fig. 16 a. CMCH and CMCL represent CMC with degrees of substitution of 1.2 and 0.7, respectively, while CN/A-CMCH and CN/A-CMCL represent hybrid CMC catalysts where -COONa groups were replaced with OOH. The CMCL/CN catalyst demonstrated a H2O2 production rate of 35.7 μmol L−1h−1, 3.5 times higher than g-C3N4, as shown in Fig. 16b.

The order of photocatalytic decomposition of H2O2 was observed as CN > CN/CMCH > CN/CMCL, as seen in Fig. 16c. CN/CMCL was the optimal sample for producing the highest amount of H2O2 with minimal photodecomposition. Importantly, the hydroxyl groups present in CMC play a critical role in regulating its interaction with C3N4 through hydrogen bonding, which ultimately influences the rate of H2O2 production, as shown in Fig. 16d. Previous studies have demonstrated that cellulose, in addition to serving as a carbon source, enhances ORR reactions for H2O2 production by acting as a proton donor. This facilitates interfacial charge transfer in hybrid photocatalysts, thereby improving photocatalytic performance [99–101].

The global coffee industry generates vast amounts of spent coffee grounds (SCG), producing approximately 6 million tons annually [102]. Waste biomass, such as SCG, holds immense potential as a valuable resource for sustainable chemical production via green catalysis. Inspired by this concept, our research group recently demonstrated the hydrothermal transformation of SCG into oxygen-enriched carbon quantum dots (OE-CQDs), which serve as excellent photocatalysts for photoinduced H2O2 production [103]. Tuning the oxygenic functional groups to achieve a high oxygen content (30.36 %) was beneficial for charge transfer during H2O2 production.

C3N4, as a polymeric carbon-based photocatalyst, has gained recent attention for H2O2 production. However, it primarily relies on organic electron donors as sacrificial reagents due to the insufficient oxidative potential of its valence band holes. Fig. 17 a illustrates a schematic of OE-CQD fabrication from SCG using a hydrothermal method. The HR-TEM image in Fig. 17b shows that OE-CQDs have a size of less than 5 nm, which may contribute to the quantum confinement effect. The oxygenic functional groups on the OE-CQD surface play a vital role in driving H₂O₂ production. The O1s spectrum of OE-CQDs-180 reveals the presence of oxygenic functionalities such as C-O-C and C=O groups (Fig. 17c).

OE-CQDs prepared under different hydrothermal conditions showed that OE-CQDs-180 exhibited the highest performance among the samples. Notably, OE-CQDs-180 demonstrated H2O2 production of 288.5 μmol g−1 h−1 without the use of an electron donor, confirming the participation of both holes and electrons in the H2O2 production process. A control reaction conducted without light irradiation or air bubbling resulted in a negligible amount of H2O2, further confirming that photo-induced oxygen reduction was the primary mechanism behind H2O2 production (Fig. 17d).

A long-term test, involving mapping the catalytic activity under dark conditions and irradiation over a 2-h period, further verified that H2O2 was produced by light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 17e, AQY of H2O2 production was measured at specific wavelengths. The highest AQY of OE-CQDs-180 was observed at 9.8 %, corresponding to 280 nm, which aligned with its absorption spectrum.

In another study, hydrothermal carbonaceous carbon (HTC-cf) derived from coffee grounds was integrated with NH2-MIL-101(Fe) for simultaneous H2O2 production and activation [104]. Beyond coffee waste, another biomass material, rutin, has been exploited to produce CDs using a microwave pyrolysis approach for H2O2 production [105]. Although OE-CQDs have demonstrated a donor-free system for H2O2 production, they are limited by their ability to absorb only a narrow range of the UV spectrum. Therefore, an upgraded system should aim to extend the optical absorption properties of SCG-derived CDs to enhance H2O2 production efficiency.

Qin et al. demonstrated the use of waste shuttlecocks to fabricate nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (NS-Cdots) for photocatalytic H2O2 production [106]. The shuttlecocks, made from natural feathers, contain keratin, a protein composed of sulfur-containing amino acids.

This provides a natural source of nitrogen, sulfur, and carbon for fabricating heteroatom-doped carbon dots during the hydrothermal treatment. The synthesis scheme for NS-Cdots is shown in Fig. 18 a. The average size of the NS-Cdots was approximately 3 nm, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis revealed the presence of graphitic carbon with [100] planes, as shown in Fig. 18b.

The S2p spectrum, shown in Fig. 18c, indicates that sulfur was predominantly present in thiophene and sulfate/sulfonate forms, which originated from the sulfur-containing keratin portions of the feathers. In contrast, nitrogen was deconvoluted into pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic forms. The synergistic effect of both nitrogen and sulfur dopants led to efficient H2O2 production in pure water without using any electron donor, with a maximum production yield of 2.06 mM h−1 g−1. However, during the cycling test, it was observed that in the second cycle, the rate of H2O2 production decreased by a factor of 2.89, reflecting the instability of the NS-doped CDs, as shown in Fig. 18d. This phenomenon was also evidenced by a significant reduction in solar-to-chemical conversion (SCC) efficiency during the initial reaction time, as seen in Fig. 18e. This finding indicates that stability issues must be considered when designing durable catalysts for H2O2 production using biomass-derived carbon dots.

In a subsequent study, NS-doped CDs were modified with amine to further improve the H2O2 production rate [107]. It is noteworthy that the band structure of biomass-derived carbon plays a significant role in driving redox reactions and governing the reaction mechanism for H2O2 production. An overview of the band structures of biomass-derived carbon-based photocatalysts and their hybrids with semiconductors is shown in Fig. 19 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of existing challenges and future prospects
ce:section.ce:para: Biomass and its derived polymers, which are abundant in nature, present promising candidates for catalytic applications. Their low cost and non-toxic characteristics offer a unique opportunity to transform waste into valuable resources. Integrating biomass-based catalysts with solar energy conversion technologies can help address the growing demand for H2O2. Developing green technologies for H2O2 production using abundant natural resources provides a compelling solution for reducing the carbon footprint and promoting a circular economy.

This review highlights recent advancements and the potential of biomass-derived carbon as a catalyst for solar-driven H2O2 production. Various types and structures of biomass materials were examined, strategies for introducing semiconducting properties into these catalysts were explored, and the reaction mechanism driving H2O2 production was investigated. Crop residues and plant-based biomasses, such as lignin, starch, and cellulose, are the most commonly used sources for H2O2 production. Additionally, biopolymers like chitosan, rutin, hesperidin, natural features, and spent coffee grounds have been explored as promising biomass resources for producing H2O2 under illumination.

While biomass and biopolymers do not behave like traditional semiconductors, making them unsuitable for photocatalysis in their pristine forms, biomass-derived carbon can exhibit semiconducting properties when properly processed. Hydrothermal carbonization of biomass is a promising strategy for transforming biomass into a functional photocatalyst. Precise tuning of temperature during hydrothermal carbonization has been shown to markedly enhance H2O2 production rates, underscoring the critical influence of hydrothermal carbonization conditions. The furan moieties formed during hydrothermal carbonization significantly impact the properties and performance of biomass-derived carbon-based photocatalysts. The conjugated furan structures effectively suppress the recombination of charge carriers through donor-acceptor complex formation during the ORR, while also extending visible light absorption.

In addition to hydrothermal carbonization of biomass, microwave-assisted pyrolysis is recognized as an alternative approach for producing carbon-based photocatalysts from biomass under mild heating conditions. Hydrothermal processing of biomass is a low-cost method for fabricating CDs, offering the unique advantage of precise surface functionality tuning with diverse functional groups. Heteroatom doping (e.g., nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus) and amine functionalization of CDs have been shown to enhance catalytic activity for biomass-derived H2O2 production. Carbon-based photocatalysts derived from biomass have also been integrated with other semiconductors to improve H2O2 production rates through effective charge separation.

Mechanistic investigations have identified the photoinduced ORR as the dominant pathway for H₂O₂ production using biomass-derived carbon. ESR investigations confirmed the formation of ·O2 − as intermediates during the ORR. In several cases, a precisely tuned valence band position facilitated H2O2 generation in pure water, eliminating the need for sacrificial electron donors. Additionally, the in-situ production of H2O2 over biomass-derived catalysts has been exploited for oxyfunctionalization reactions, activating reactive oxygen species. Our previous work and several other groups have highlighted that oxygen functionalities in carbon based photocatalyst can play pivotal role in promoting H2O2 production [44,103,108]. In previous findings C-O-C have been identified as most active functionalities for favouring H2O2 production [108]. The role of various oxygen groups on the surface of catalyst promoting H2O2 production has been highlighted in Fig. 20 . These findings open up new possibilities for utilizing waste biomass in photocatalytic applications. Table 1 provides a summary and performance comparison of biomass-derived photocatalysts for solar H2O2 production in term of production rate, AQY and SSC efficiency.

Despite significant progress in the emerging field of photocatalytic H2O2 production using biomass-derived carbon, research remains in its early stages. Several critical challenges must be addressed to fully exploit biomass as a valuable resource for H2O2 production under illumination with breakthrough performance. The key challenges are as follows:
i)
Biomass-driven H2O2 production typically results in lower solar-to- H2O2 efficiency and inferior quantum yields. Light-harvesting efficiency must be improved through the development of hybrid catalysts and the adoption of appropriate co-catalysts.
ii)
Catalyst instability is a major drawback, as biomass-derived carbon can undergo photodegradation, leading to a gradual loss of activity for H2O2 production. Surface modification and structural optimization during hydrothermal carbonization are essential for enhancing catalyst stability.
iii)
Biomass-based photocatalysts exhibit inferior charge transport capabilities, and high recombination tendencies can be mitigated by separating redox centers and promoting effective electron-hole pair separation.
iv)
CDs derived from biomass show insufficient light absorption, typically limited to the UV range. Bandgap engineering of CDs should be implemented to extend light absorption into the visible region, boosting their performance for H2O2 production.
v)
Insufficient oxidative potential in biomass-derived carbon often necessitates the use of sacrificial electron donors to drive the ORR. Particular attention should be given to tuning the thermodynamic potential of biomass-derived carbon to eliminate the need for electron donors.
vi)
Biomass-derived carbon generates lower photocurrents than oxide semiconductors due to its inferior electrical conductivity. This can be addressed by regulating the relative proportions of sp 2 and sp³ carbons, introducing heteroatoms, and controlling the degree of graphitization during hydrothermal carbonization.
vii)
Biomass derived carbon based photocatalysts exhibits poor dynamics of photoexcited charge carriers with inferior life time. It arises because of poor electronic transportation at molecular level and high degree of defects and poor crystallinity. Careful control of hydrothermal carbonization can improve the crystallinity and hence dynamics of excited charge carriers.
Addressing these challenges will facilitate the fabrication of efficient and stable biomass-derived catalysts for solar H2O2 production.

Paskalis Sahaya Murphin Kumar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Conceptualization. Do-Yeon Lee: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Haein Cho: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Sang Hwan Son: Investigation. Jungho Jae: Investigation. Jung Rae Kim: Investigation. Oh Seok Kwon: Writing – review & editing. Zeeshan Haider: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Hyoung-il Kim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
